records.title: DMS Plan and Repository Delivery
records.summary: A Data Management and Sharing Plan is only useful if it matches what actually happens to the data. Research teams usually know they need a DMS Plan and know they will eventually submit to a repository. The harder part is the messy middle: gathering, understanding, restructuring, documenting, validating, and delivering the data on time. The work here follows that practical workflow from project systems to repository delivery.

records.title: Sutton SignWriting Infrastructure Initiative
records.summary: Sutton SignWriting already exists as a global system. What it lacks is stable long-term support for its standards, tools, data, and public resources. SignWriting already has real tools, standards, and public materials, but too much of the maintenance and coordination still depends on a thin support base. The goal is to give that work a stronger home, clearer partners, and more reliable follow-through.

records.title: The Science of Sutton SignWriting
records.summary: This work begins with a central claim: language is not tied to sound. It is a structured brain and meaning system that can operate through speech or vision. The science lane asks what changes when language is not tied to sound and signed language gains writing of its own. It begins with Language Without Sound, extends through The Shape of Writing, and then continues into references, technical notes, evidence notes, and future studies on visual language, literacy, cognition, writing, and research support.

records.title: About ISWA 2010
records.summary: ISWA 2010 is the symbol inventory for modern Sutton SignWriting computing, organized as a stable alphabet rather than an open-ended drawing set. ISWA 2010 organizes the alphabet by category, group, base, fill, and rotation. It is the symbol layer used by fonts, editors, palettes, rendering, and Formal SignWriting encodings.
keywords: Categories divide the alphabet into major regions such as Hands, Movement, Head and Faces, Body, and Punctuation
Groups organize related symbol families within each category
Each base identifies one panel within the alphabet
Each panel is arranged as 6 fill values by 16 rotation values
ISWA 2010 defines one bounded inventory for compatible Sutton SignWriting work
The alphabet is used across fonts, palettes, websites, rendering tools, and formal encodings
Palm facing, fill, rotation, and related contrasts remain part of symbol identity
Compatible fonts redraw the alphabet without redefining symbol identity
Changes to the inventory produce forks rather than minor implementation variations
Formal SignWriting defines the structured sign model built from that layer
FSW and SWU are equivalent encodings of the formal model, not replacements for the alphabet
Rendering, search, and tooling all depend on stable symbol identity
Category: top-level division of the alphabet
Group: related family within a category
Panel: 6 by 16 matrix of related symbol forms for one base identity
Base: underlying symbol identity for a panel, referenced by a key such as S100
Fill: one axis of variation across the panel, with six values
Rotation: the second axis of variation across the panel, with sixteen values
Symbol key: complete FSW key combining base, fill, and rotation, such as S10000
Categories, groups, base symbols, and the fill-and-rotation matrix.

records.title: Licensing and Reuse
records.summary: Clear licenses for software, fonts, publications, and related research outputs. Those materials are meant to be used, studied, implemented, shared, and cited, but they do not all belong under the same license. Software is licensed as software. Fonts are licensed as fonts. Essays, slides, and companion documents are licensed as publications. The ISWA 2010 and Formal SignWriting Internet-Drafts, released through the IETF, also remain part of the public record and were released for reference, examination, implementation, evaluation, and unlimited distribution. If a package, font, or document includes its own more specific license statement, follow that specific statement.

records.parentTitle: Brazil as the Empirical Center of SignWriting
keywords: Brazil as the Empirical Center of SignWriting
records.contentExcerpt: Brazil as the Empirical Center of SignWriting Documented institutional density, research opportunity, and why Brazil matters Brazil should not be treated as a side example in SignWriting conversations. It is the strongest documented empirical center currently visible in the publi...
records.searchText: brazil as the empirical center of signwriting essay essay for brazil as the empirical center of signwriting a compact evidence brief showing why brazil is the strongest documented empirical center for signwriting use instruction and future research documented institutional density research opportunity and why brazil matters series sutton signwriting infrastructure initiative brazil as the empirical center of signwriting essay sutton signwriting infrastructure initiative brazil as the empirical center of signwriting essay brazil as the empirical center of signwriting brazil as the empirical center of signwriting documented institutional density research opportunity and why brazil matters brazil should not be treated as a side example in signwriting conversations it is the strongest documented empirical center currently visible in the public record that statement does not depend on claiming that every detail has already been counted or that every part of the brazilian story has been fully told it depends on something simpler brazil shows repeated institutional use rather than isolated anecdote brazil has both school based and university based signwriting activity brazil has a substantial research record brazil provides a realistic starting point for future literacy cognition corpus and infrastructure work the public signwriting record also shows that this did not appear suddenly the brazil archive on signwriting org links historical materials classrooms dictionaries literature courses and university references and the symposium archive preserves a long run of brazil centered presentations in education research software and literacy at the current public count the symposium archive alone includes 37 brazil related presentations from 2014 to 2024 why brazil deserves separate treatment much of the international discussion around signwriting still treats use cases as scattered examples brazil no longer fits that pattern the public record already shows enough institutional density to justify treating brazil as a major empirical center in its own right that matters because it changes the starting point for scientific and institutional conversations instead of asking whether signwriting could ever support real educational and research use a more serious question becomes possible how should the strongest existing signwriting ecosystem be documented studied and supported the 2018 documented floor a useful baseline comes from the 2018 accounting attached to the brazilian sign language wikipedia project that document reported 21 schools and universities across 13 brazilian states with over 1 000 students the safest use of this figure is as a documented floor it should not be presented as a complete national census it should be presented as the minimum institutional density already visible in a documented project record that is already enough to matter it also sits inside a longer visible trajectory the public record on signwriting org traces brazilian activity back to the 1990s and later connects it to signnet ufsc online courses school based literacy work and continuing symposium activity why the institutional shape matters the documented network included both deaf schools and universities this is one reason the brazil case is so important it was not merely one classroom or one short lived pilot site the 2018 documentation named university and school settings including the signwriting research group at the federal university of santa catarina the federal university of cear in partnership with deaf school work in fortaleza the federal university of maranh o in s o lu s deaf school use in places such as santa maria and ipia that institutional spread suggests a broader ecosystem of teaching training and research it also aligns with a second layer of evidence from the symposium archive a 2014 symposium presentation on brazilian deaf education reported that since 2006 signwriting had formed a 180 hour part of the letras libras curriculum at ufsc and that the first two distance offerings in 2006 and 2010 reached more than 1 200 deaf and hearing students across 17 brazilian states plus the federal district that claim should still be read historically and in context but it shows that brazil s institutional role is deeper than a handful of isolated workshops what this means scientifically brazil matters scientifically because it provides what many future signwriting studies will need actual learner populations actual educational settings repeated institutional environments a base for corpora teaching materials and longitudinal observation this does not by itself prove every educational or cognitive claim one might want to make it does something more important at this stage it makes the research program realistic brazil gives signwriting research a concrete empirical center rather than a purely theoretical future the symposium archive reinforces this point brazilian presentations have addressed reading literacy online teaching phonological transcription bilingual schooling parallel corpora television and public communication school based implementation and curricular integration that range suggests not only use but a real research and practice ecosystem by 2024 the public record also includes studies of deaf academic readers linguistic comfort and cultural identification when reading libras texts in signwriting along with curricular and literary presentations that make the brazilian case look increasingly mature rather than experimental what this means strategically brazil also matters strategically it provides a real evidence base that remains under read in english language academia that means the next useful work is not limited to defending the possibility of signed writing in the abstract it includes updating the institutional map synthesizing brazilian work into english forming research partnerships building shared datasets and infrastructure designing studies around actual signwriting users rather than hypothetical ones in that sense brazil is not merely an encouraging case it is the strongest current base for building a serious empirical and infrastructural program around signwriting limits of the claim the claim here is intentionally narrower than a full national history it does not claim that the 2018 accounting is complete that all current brazilian programs have been verified one by one that every degree pathway or curriculum requirement involving signwriting has already been mapped that the brazilian record alone settles every literacy question a full historical and institutional account of signwriting in brazil would be much longer than this account but that does not weaken the present point the documented floor is already large enough to justify serious attention what should happen next the strongest next steps are practical produce an updated national accounting verify current institutional use program by program where needed synthesize brazilian research for english language audiences use brazilian institutional density as the basis for future literacy and reading studies connect research planning with durable signwriting infrastructure brazil therefore deserves to be treated not merely as a successful chapter in signwriting history but as the clearest current empirical center for what signwriting has already become and what it can support next for a denser presentation by presentation review of the symposium record see symposium review md

records.searchText: country ambassadors essay essay for country ambassadors a volunteer first country ambassador model for trusted local outreach translation and early relationship building country level outreach translation and local relationship building series sutton signwriting infrastructure initiative country ambassadors essay sutton signwriting infrastructure initiative country ambassadors essay country ambassadors country ambassadors a lightweight country ambassador model can give the project trusted local help with outreach translation and early relationship building in each country why this role is needed international outreach and follow up are difficult to centralize well each country has different university structures languages and communication norms funding realities and economic conditions signwriting communities leaders and institutional pathways a country ambassador model creates local relationship capacity without expecting one central team to understand every context on its own role summary a country ambassador is a trusted local volunteer who helps connect the sutton signwriting initiative with universities educators researchers and community leaders in a specific country this begins as a volunteer service role with the expectation that as funding becomes available the role may evolve into a paid stipend position for services rendered and to support local signwriting work core goals country ambassadors help identify universities schools and other useful contacts make outreach more culturally and linguistically appropriate explain local conditions needs and constraints build trust with educational and research partners gather letters of support usage statements and collaboration interest strengthen the case for grants gifts and donations through the university of iowa typical responsibilities responsibilities may include mapping relevant universities schools programs and community organizations identifying faculty administrators and local champions helping translate or adapt outreach materials making introductions and supporting early conversations sharing local feedback about feasibility priorities and concerns documenting visible signwriting use and impact in the country helping identify possible future collaborators advisors or donors what ambassadors are not country ambassadors are not expected to make legal or financial commitments on behalf of the project independently negotiate institutional agreements serve as payroll or payment intermediaries represent the university of iowa in a formal administrative capacity unless specifically authorized their role is relational informational and community building volunteer first model the initial ambassador structure should be simple start with volunteer appointments define country scope clearly agree on reasonable expected activities provide shared messaging and outreach materials recognize contributions visibly and respectfully this keeps the barrier to entry low while building real international support capacity path to stipends as institutional support grows ambassadors may be eligible for modest stipends tied to clearly defined services such as translation or localization work outreach coordination institutional engagement support event or meeting facilitation documentation of national use cases and partnerships stipends should be introduced only when administrative and funding pathways are clear because economic conditions vary widely across countries compensation should be handled thoughtfully rather than through a one size fits all model selection criteria strong ambassadors are likely to have some combination of trusted standing in local signwriting or deaf education communities strong communication skills familiarity with local universities or institutional networks ability to work across language and cultural boundaries reliability in documenting conversations and follow up initial countries a practical first cohort could include brazil mexico south korea additional countries where active signwriting use and institutional interest are already visible support needed from the central team to make the model work the central project should provide a short ambassador description a simple onboarding note outreach templates that can be localized a shared contact and follow up tracker a clear explanation of how ambassador work connects to grants gifts donations and partnerships recommended next step recruit a small pilot group of ambassadors in 3 to 5 countries and test the model before expanding further

and coordinators project systems such as redcap spreadsheets and csv exports external tools such as nih toolbox imaging or technical data sources outside entities that hold or generate data the way to understand the work is through the lifecycle source data raw structured data derivatives repository delivery source data source data is the original material collected across the project it may include redcap exports nih toolbox files spreadsheets csv exports technical system outputs files from collaborators or vendors source data is often broad and heterogeneous it may contain more fields analysis or sharing in its original form raw structured data raw structured data is the cleaned and organized form prepared for analysis and later sharing this stage usually requires consistent naming with review quick restructuring and macros csv workflows may help with portability and repository preparation bash and python may become necessary when the work is too repetitive or too complex for manual handling the goal is to shape and support these workflows so they become clearer more repeatable more aligned with sharing goals less fragile under deadline pressure repository delivery as real labor repository delivery is where many plans become real or fall apart for repositories such as nda the work often includes matching local variables to the repository dictionary clarifying required versus optional structure preparing files in the right shape correcting validation failures coordinating recurring submission cycles the dms plan is operational not merely descriptive a good dms plan should anticipate what local systems produce what transformations are required what repository constraints are likely to matter where labor and responsibility will fall the point is to do more than discuss those questions and actually carry the work through timing and project pressure dms work is not evenly distributed across a project pressure often spikes around biannual repository cycles annual review cycles urgent analysis requests sudden sharing needs a team may appear to be doing fine during quiet periods and then struggle when a real submission or deliverable is due that is one reason this work matters so much this kind of support can be valuable both in planning ahead while the project still has time helping the project respond effectively when pressure rises two ways this records.contentExcerpt: Developer Notes What to use now and what not to assume Current recommendation For current Sutton SignWriting software and data work: use FSW as canonical production text use SWU when you need a Unicode-oriented isomorphic form use ISWA 2010 as the symbol reference layer do not as...
records.searchText: developer notes essay essay for developer notes implementation guidance for developers working with sutton signwriting data conversion symbol identity and unicode facing workflows what to use now and what not to assume series unicode and signwriting developer notes essay unicode and signwriting developer notes essay developer notes developer notes what to use now and what not to assume current recommendation for current sutton signwriting software and data work use fsw as canonical production text use swu when you need a unicode oriented isomorphic form use iswa 2010 as the symbol reference layer do not assume official unicode signwriting is a drop in replacement for the sutton signwriting resources if the production judgment changes in the future because unicode support genuinely becomes sufficient and compatible then the recommendation can change too why fsw remains canonical fsw remains the most practical canonical encoding because it is ascii stable for storage and transfer practical for regex and parsing already integrated into production tools and datasets independent of official unicode signwriting limitations if a workflow already uses fsw successfully that is not a temporary embarrassment it is the current production reality production reality should outweigh theoretical convenience when the two diverge it also means developers should resist pressure to migrate simply because a standards label looks cleaner on paper why swu matters swu matters because it gives an isomorphic unicode oriented view of the same model it is useful for unicode facing inspection one dimensional display of encoded sign structure interchange where unicode text is expected current ai and dataset workflows that benefit from a more visibly structured unicode string swu is not official unicode it is the unicode oriented representation supported by the sutton signwriting resources this distinction is one of the main things developers tend to miss on first contact what not to assume about official unicode signwriting do not assume that official unicode signwriting names characters in a way that automatically preserves stable symbols preserves the full iswa symbol model cleanly can represent full written signs in a compatible way can replace fsw or swu without redesign can sort or collate the way production workflows need can reuse the sutton signwriting production fonts without mismatch can solve layout by adding a font alone it encodes much of the character set that is not the same as preserving stable writer selected symbols it also does not currently provide a complete compatible written sign solution in the facial system the model is not simply writer selects symbols and places them instead a sequence is interpreted by the font and arranged into a rendered face that means part of the resulting facial arrangement depends on font behavior rather than direct writer selected iswa symbols and authored placement that means a developer should not infer from the existence of official characters that a full application stack is already settled this is the same mistake many outsiders make at the public level but it causes more damage when it appears inside software decisions why this is not only a rendering problem rendering is part of the issue but not the main issue the larger problem is interoperability developers need stable writer selected symbols stable symbol identity reliable storage reliable interchange searchability predictable sorting stable relation to existing datasets fonts alone do not provide those things they also do not settle which final facial forms exist as stable shared symbols when the model pushes composition into font interpretation this is the practical reason developer discussions drift quickly from fonts into encoding and conversion practical storage advice if you are designing a current system store fsw internally unless you have a strong reason not to derive swu when you need unicode oriented display or export treat iswa symbol identity as stable and shared treat rendering and style as a separate layer keep conversion routines explicit and testable if you are exposing multiple encodings in one codebase make the boundaries visible what is canonical what is interchange what is legacy or compatibility support what is experimental practical migration advice if someone asks whether to migrate existing sutton signwriting data to official unicode signwriting the safe answer is not without a clear compatibility study first before any migration ask are stable writer selected symbols preserved across the full system can the full written sign be represented without loss can existing sorting be preserved can current fonts and renderers still work can search and parsing stay stable can round tripping be guaranteed if the answer is not clearly yes migration is not yet justified the burden of proof should stay on the migration not on the existing production stack practical ai and data advice for ai and larger dataset work the key constraint now is less the existence of a usable encoding and more the size and quality of the data in current practice fsw remains a strong canonical form swu is also usable for ai oriented data preparation and model input this shows the sutton signwriting production stack is still computationally relevant unicode incompleteness is still a serious standards problem but it is not the same thing as computational impossibility bottom line in practice fsw for canonical production text swu for supported unicode oriented interchange and inspection official unicode signwriting only with caution and only with a clear understanding that it does not currently replace the sutton signwriting production stack a note about sutton signwriting unicode8 the package sutton signwriting unicode8 is still useful and worth linking for developers its role is to process official unicode 8 signwriting characters where they exist and to bridge them with fsw and swu inside javascript tooling useful links source documentation distribution issues real software still has to deal with official unicode signwriting even when it is not the preferred production path describe such packages as compatibility tooling bridge tooling inspection tooling

records.contentExcerpt: Sutton SignWriting as Research Infrastructure Executive Brief Executive Frame Sutton SignWriting is not only a writing system. It is also an existing body of tools, standards, data, and public resources that could support much more research if it had a stronger institutional home...
records.searchText: executive brief essay essay for executive brief a concise forwardable entry that states the mission support gap and opportunity around sutton signwriting sutton signwriting as research infrastructure series sutton signwriting infrastructure initiative executive brief essay sutton signwriting infrastructure initiative executive brief essay executive brief sutton signwriting as research infrastructure executive brief executive frame sutton signwriting is not only a writing system it is also an existing body of tools standards data and public resources that could support much more research if it had a stronger institutional home mission statement the mission of this project is to give sutton signwriting a stable academic home so its existing standards tools datasets and research pathways can be maintained studied preserved and extended in practical terms that means moving from informal stewardship of a globally used system to stronger institutional support that can sustain research preservation and future growth what sutton signwriting is sutton signwriting is a system for writing signed languages using a structured visual script it is not reducible to sound and visually structured expressive enough to represent full signed languages used internationally across education research and community contexts supported by a technical ecosystem of tools encodings and data unlike glossing or transcription systems signwriting is not an annotation layer on spoken language it is a direct written representation of signed language existing asset base the signwriting ecosystem already includes active functioning tools standards sites and datasets under continuous technical stewardship this is not a speculative or early stage system it is a mature distributed platform that has developed over more than two decades of use existing assets include long running public domains such as signwriting org signbank org and related sites production tools such as signpuddle and signmaker used for writing and dictionary development formal encoding standards including iswa 2010 fsw and swu together with associated fonts multi language software libraries in javascript python and php publicly distributed packages and developer tooling accumulated datasets dictionaries and written materials this matters because a host institution would not be starting from zero it would be stabilizing and extending an existing system that is already in active use the current gap what is missing is not the idea the community or the software what is missing is stable coordinated support at present the ecosystem depends on distributed maintenance across individuals uneven documentation and onboarding limited institutional anchoring fragile continuity for long term stewardship this creates risk for data preservation tool continuity research use long term growth the current gap is not the absence of work it is the absence of stable institutional support for work that already exists and is actively maintained why this matters for research treating signwriting as shared research support rather than only as a niche language project changes what becomes possible a stronger support structure would support corpus development for signed languages reproducible datasets and shared standards integration with machine learning and computational linguistics research into visual language processing and literacy cross linguistic comparison and documentation without infrastructure these remain isolated efforts with infrastructure they become scalable research programs the strongest current empirical base for that work is brazil the public record now supports treating brazil as the clearest documented center of signwriting use teaching and research rather than only as one encouraging example among many it is also the strongest currently documented large scale real world context for studying signed language literacy through signwriting south korea adds a different kind of strength it provides a current official institutional proof point through the korean sign language nuri dictionary showing that signwriting already appears inside public dictionary infrastructure connected to a national language institution that matters because it gives the infrastructure case a real evidence base real institutions real learner populations real written materials real research and curricular history why now several factors make this a timely opportunity growing interest in multimodal and non speech language processing increased emphasis on data sharing and reusable datasets maturing technical tooling around signwriting a visible if under supported global user base in addition the technical foundation has reached a level of maturity where it can support broader research use including corpus development computational analysis and integration with modern data workflows the research path is also clearer than before reading and literacy studies brazil grounded empirical work south korea as a current institutional proof point and infrastructure plus dataset development can now be described as one connected program rather than as separate hopes this creates a narrow window where institutional support can turn existing work into a stable grant ready program what a pi or host institution would unlock a host institution would be building on an existing technical and data foundation not initiating a new system with a stable home the project could transition from informal stewardship to a more durable institutional program this would enable coordinated development of tools and standards systematic documentation and onboarding preparation of grant proposals grounded in real assets partnerships across linguistics education computer science and neuroscience translation and access to especially important international signwriting works long term preservation planning the role of a pi or host lab would be to provide institutional anchoring guide research framing and publication lead or co lead grant development support integration into existing research programs closing the most compelling framing is simple sutton signwriting already exists as a working globally used system with software data and standards what it lacks is stable institutional support with the right home it becomes a research and technical resource that others can rely on build on and fund with confidence

records.contentExcerpt: Formal SignWriting The two-part word, sign text, and the core model Formal SignWriting is a technical model for sign text that can be stored, searched, checked, and processed by software without losing the written form. Its central idea is simple: a sign can be modeled as a two-p...
records.searchText: formal signwriting essay essay for formal signwriting canonical opening statement of the two part word and of sign text that software can store search check and process the two part word sign text and the core model series formal signwriting formal signwriting essay formal signwriting formal signwriting essay formal signwriting formal signwriting the two part word sign text and the core model formal signwriting is a technical model for sign text that can be stored searched checked and processed by software without losing the written form its central idea is simple a sign can be modeled as a two part word of time and space in practical terms a full sign always has a spatial signbox it may also carry a temporal prefix that word therefore contains an optional temporal prefix which preserves a one dimensional symbol sequence a required spatial signbox which preserves a two dimensional arrangement of writing symbols the opening task is to define the core model clearly enough that every later layer stays legible core claim formal signwriting should be understood as a way to write signs as text that software can work with a grammar for valid strings a bridge between visible writing and software handling a framework that supports search rendering and transformation a long term implementation record for developers researchers and maintainers minimal vocabulary the core vocabulary is small term meaning sign a required spatial signbox optionally preceded by a temporal prefix temporal prefix the optional sequence preserving part used mainly for sorting and sometimes for analysis spatial signbox the required composition preserving part fsw or swu plain text encodings of the model with fsw commonly canonical in processing and swu supported for unicode oriented interchange style string optional presentation markup not canonical sign text if these definitions are clear the rest of the model becomes much easier to hold in view the two part word the core structure is a two part word an optional temporal prefix for sequence sensitive information a required spatial signbox for composed written form that separation matters because it preserves two different aspects of writing at once sequence spatial composition this is not a contradiction it is the design solution if the model preserved only sequence too much of the written form would be flattened away if it preserved only spatial arrangement too much would become hard to store search and process the two part word is the move that keeps both a worked example consider this fsw sign it can be read in two parts the first line is the temporal prefix it begins with a it preserves a sequence of symbol keys it primarily supports sorting it can also carry linguistic analysis written according to a theory the second line is the spatial signbox it begins with the signbox marker m it establishes a signbox coordinate it places concrete symbols at concrete coordinates this example is useful because it makes the architecture visible the word is still a string but the string is carrying both sequence and signbox sequence sorting and theory the temporal prefix should not be described as derivative markup extracted automatically from the signbox it is written by an author according to a theory of sorting or analysis in current practice val s sign spelling guidelines is the most prevalent theory that matters because the sequence is primarily used for sorting the sequence can also carry linguistic analysis the sequence is theory based rather than mechanically recoverable from two dimensional space alone formal signwriting does not force one sorting theory that theory agnosticism is a strength but it has a practical consequence if multiple sequence theories are mixed in one collection sorting can be performed in multiple incompatible ways which reduces the value of the sort technical consequences once the two part word is accepted several technical layers become clearer fsw and swu as plain text encodings character sets and conversion symbol and coordinate models grammar and token patterns search and query language rendering and layout styling as optional presentation markup implementation and maintenance strategy these layers are easier to teach separately but they remain consequences of the same model the dependency can be stated more compactly layer what it receives from the core model encoding one stable text form geometry one explicit signbox arrangement grammar one valid structural account search one searchable text form rendering one source for visible output practice one stable implementation target that table is useful because it shows that the two part word is not only a description of signs it is the starting condition for the entire architecture why the two part word comes first the two part word is not just one idea among many it is the center of the whole technical architecture the encoding layer preserves it the spatial model explains it the grammar validates it the search layer exploits it the rendering layer realizes it if this move is not clear later layers feel disconnected if it is clear the whole model becomes legible this is why the model should feel settled at the center even when the ecosystem around it continues to grow core point formal signwriting works because it lets one textual form preserve two things at once the optional theory based sequence of symbols the spatial composition of the written sign that is what makes written sign text both faithful to the writing and usable in software

records.searchText: fsw and swu essay essay for fsw and swu plain text encodings explicit character inventories conversion and canonical text boundaries plain text encodings character sets and conversion series formal signwriting fsw and swu essay formal signwriting fsw and swu essay fsw and swu fsw and swu plain text encodings character sets and conversion formal signwriting needs a clear account of its plain text encodings this entry focuses on fsw as an ascii oriented encoding swu as a unicode oriented encoding character sets and conversion canonical text versus optional markup fsw and swu fsw and swu should be treated as plain text encodings of formal signwriting they are two technical ways to carry the same underlying model forward into processing storage and interchange they do not represent two different theories of the writing system they represent two character strategies for the same architecture from a stewardship perspective the distinction should be stated plainly fsw is commonly the canonical internal form for processing storage and indexing swu is supported for input output conversion and unicode oriented workflows script code and language tags sgnw is the iso 15924 script code for signwriting a language tag such as ase sgnw identifies american sign language written in the signwriting script that tag does not tell you whether the text is serialized in fsw or swu the language tag identifies language plus script the encoding choice belongs in the surrounding format schema protocol profile or technical documentation this is why both fsw and swu can represent the same ase sgnw content how an fsw symbol key works an fsw symbol key is not opaque a key such as s10000 can be read as s symbol key marker 100 base symbol 0 fill 0 rotation this matters because base fill and rotation are preserved across conversion the base identifies the symbol family and is the part used for range tests such as writing movement punctuation hcenter or vcenter character inventory at a glance the technical inventory can be summarized this way description fsw swu sequence marker a u 1d800 signbox markers b l m r u 1d801 to u 1d804 numbers 250 to 749 u 1d80c hosting configuration uptime and monitoring content preservation and migration planning this is already a real web infrastructure portfolio server and platform infrastructure the ecosystem currently depends on a combination of legacy signwriting servers active production servers static and dynamic web hosting environments these systems support signpuddle and dictionary services signwriting org and archival content developer resources and documentation data access and downloads key characteristics include long running systems with historical continuity a mixed technology stack including php javascript python and static hosting evolving architecture with ongoing redevelopment in platform v3 immediate operational concerns include backup and disaster recovery migration planning for legacy systems documentation of deployment and configuration reduction of single person operational dependency this is exactly the kind of infrastructure that becomes more valuable as its maintenance risk becomes more visible concrete operational pressure the continuity risk is no longer only theoretical recent international work has already exposed where the pressure is felt in south korea preparation for the february 6 2025 launch of the korean sign language nuri dictionary depended on signpuddle 2 on the legacy server to author signs for a public institutional dictionary that work appears to have pushed the old system hard enough to expose scaling weaknesses in save operations and concurrent use direct support was needed during the launch period including two days of vacation time taken to keep the work moving in brazil prior outages were felt immediately in an active user community that depends on the system for ongoing work more recently repeated bot pressure and cpu heavy requests have continued to cause temporary service interruptions on the legacy server these incidents matter because they show that fragile support is already interrupting real international work not merely raising abstract maintenance concerns software and code infrastructure the signwriting ecosystem includes a substantial body of maintained code across multiple languages primary development is organized through the sutton signwriting github organization and related repositories including core libraries in javascript python and php rendering and font systems web components and client applications api and platform development tools such as signmaker signpuddle viewers and related utilities key characteristics include cross language implementation open source licensing active development and maintenance production use in tools websites and research contexts this is a mature technical foundation not a prototype environment data and corpus assets one of the most significant infrastructure components is the accumulated signwriting data this includes signpuddle dictionaries and datasets spml signpuddle markup language files millions of sign entries across languages structured lexical and textual resources literature collections including educational and religious texts key characteristics include community generated and internationally sourced data accumulation over roughly two decades preservation through multiple format migrations partial normalization and structure already present this dataset represents a rare large scale corpus of written signed language immediate support concerns include long term preservation format standardization metadata and indexing controlled access pathways for research use for an academic host that is not just a maintenance burden it is also a serious research asset standards and encoding infrastructure the technical foundation of signwriting includes formal encoding systems and specifications international signwriting alphabet 2010 iswa 2010 formal signwriting in ascii fsw signwriting in unicode swu associated fonts and rendering systems these systems enable machine readable signwriting support searching sorting and processing integrate with modern web and programming environments together they represent a complete and stable encoding framework for written signed language stewardship responsibilities include maintaining compatibility across formats documenting specifications supporting implementation across tools and languages preserving the link between formal standards and production systems this standards layer is one of the clearest reasons to treat the work as long term scholarly and technical infrastructure continuity and responsibility current responsibility follows a recognizable lineage valerie sutton as originator of sutton signwriting and foundational system design steve slevinski as technical steward encoding architect and infrastructure maintainer a designated next generation technical path for future continuity this continuity includes technical knowledge transfer operational responsibilities across domains servers and code preservation of standards datasets and public facing systems the issue is not that continuity is absent the issue is that the current support model is still too informal for infrastructure of this scope and importance risk profile with a clearer internal view the primary risks can be stated more directly concentration of operational knowledge dependence on a very small number of maintainers incomplete documentation of systems and workflows limited institutional redundancy long term preservation risk for data domains and services these are typical risks for infrastructure that has grown in value faster than the support around it that framing matters it means the situation is not unusual because the work is weak it is unusual because the work has become significant without yet receiving the stronger host structure it now requires why this matters for a host institution this level of asset maturity changes the institutional question a host institution would not be funding an idea starting from zero being asked to invent relevance after the fact it would be stabilizing and extending an existing globally used infrastructure system inheriting a research ready and partnership ready technical base making preservation maintenance and future grants more credible this reduces startup cost and increases grant readiness collaboration potential research applicability educational and public impact value relationship to platform v3 platform v3 is the next architectural layer not the starting point the existing infrastructure already includes domains systems code data and standards platform v3 is the opportunity to modernize the service layer improve user registration and access patterns support institutional and educational use more effectively create a better base for ai translation and research workflows in other words platform v3 extends the infrastructure it does not create it from nothing bottom line signwriting infrastructure already exists across domains servers software systems datasets standards the current challenge is not creation in plain terms it is stabilization documentation and institutionalization that means keeping the systems running documenting them clearly and giving them a stronger institutional home recommended institutional reading the most important conclusion is simple sutton signwriting has already passed the threshold from interesting project to real infrastructure the remaining question is whether that infrastructure will continue under an informal maintenance model or be given the stronger academic technical and administrative home that its current scale now warrants

records.contentExcerpt: Language Without Sound Brain networks, pathway expansion, literacy, and the case for SignWriting infrastructure Most people assume that language depends on sound. It does not. records.searchText: language without sound essay essay for language without sound entry 1 in the science of sutton signwriting series brain networks pathway expansion literacy and the case for signwriting infrastructure series the science of sutton signwriting language without sound essay the science of sutton signwriting language without sound essay language without sound language without sound brain networks pathway expansion literacy and the case for signwriting infrastructure most people assume that language depends on sound it does not the human brain does not require speech in order to build language it requires accessible linguistic input this does not mean that actual language use is disembodied or that modality makes no difference it means that human language is not reducible to sound and that core linguistic organization can be reached through different embodied modalities the brain is waiting for language not for one privileged sensory channel a second claim follows if language is not reducible to sound then literacy depends on whether a language has durable readable and writable pathways that is where signed languages face an infrastructure gap in durable read write pathways and where signwriting becomes important a pathway model the central distinction is not speech versus non speech it is pathway type human language and literacy depend on at least four kinds of pathways language input pathways how language reaches the brain language output pathways how language is expressed literacy input pathways how language is read as durable symbolic text literacy output pathways how language is written as durable symbolic text examples of language output include speaking signing handwriting typing braille writing tapping morse code these outputs matter but they do not define language by themselves language is defined by the systems that organize meaning structure and expression in the brain two developmental phases language comes first the first major developmental event is not reading it is the formation of a language system a preliterate brain develops language when it has accessible linguistic input repeated social interaction usable expressive output at this stage the brain is building and integrating pathways between perception action memory and language networks this can happen through different sensory routes spoken language in spoken language development auditory input enters through hearing auditory systems preprocess patterns language networks organize structure and meaning output is often spoken through the mouth and vocal tract signed language in signed language development visual input includes handshape movement facial expression and spatial structure visual systems preprocess those patterns language networks organize structure and meaning output is often signing through the hands face and body at the language level this is not secondary or lesser it is a full language pathway tactile language access touch also matters but careful distinctions are necessary tactile signing is a language access pathway used by many deafblind people braille is typically a tactile writing system rather than a separate language these should not be collapsed into one category the safest general statement is human language pathways can be reached through audition vision and touch literacy comes second literacy does not create language from nothing it expands an already linguistic system by adding durable symbolic routes reading and writing change what becomes possible language can be revisited language can be revised language can be compared across time language can be searched and reused language can be built into corpora datasets and research tools literacy is therefore more than storage it is the development of durable symbolic input and output print literacy print adds visual symbolic input for reading manual symbolic output through handwriting or typing braille literacy braille adds tactile symbolic input for reading tactile or typed output for writing braille is one of the clearest demonstrations that literacy does not require vision the question for signed languages the key question is whether signed languages also have a true written layer a readable symbolic input pathway a writable symbolic output pathway that question is more consequential than it first appears without such pathways signed languages may be fully linguistic but they remain at a structural disadvantage in literacy research and long term technical development that is not a minor gap it is a missing layer of language infrastructure without writable pathways signed languages face extra friction in education research and technology not because of the language itself but because of missing infrastructure this is not a claim of linguistic incompleteness it is a claim about missing durable symbolic pathways comparative cases the science becomes clearer when the comparison is strategic rather than exhaustive case 1 hearing child with spoken language and print literacy language input auditory speech language output spoken language literacy input visual print literacy output handwriting or typing language develops first and print literacy adds a durable symbolic route later case 2 deaf child with early sign language language input visual signed language language output signing literacy input often print in the surrounding spoken language literacy output often writing in the surrounding spoken language this case shows that language does not depend on sound while a native facing writing system may still be missing case 3 blind child with spoken language and braille literacy language input spoken language through hearing language output speech literacy input tactile braille literacy output braille writing or typing this case shows that literacy does not depend on vision case 4 deaf child with early sign language plus signwriting language input visual signed language language output signing literacy input visual reading of signed language through signwriting literacy output direct writing of signed language through signwriting the issue is whether signed language can gain readable and writable pathways within the language itself contrast case language present but not fully accessible when language is socially present but not sufficiently accessible to the learner early development may be unstable or incomplete later literacy may be built on a weak language base literacy cannot simply substitute for missing early language access this contrast keeps the difference between language in the environment and language perceived by the learner in clear view the infrastructure gap spoken languages usually have mature writing systems blind readers can develop durable tactile literacy through braille signed languages by contrast have often lacked widely adopted durable native facing written infrastructure instead they have often depended on video as the main durable record specialized transcription systems for research and analysis both are valuable neither is the same as ordinary readable and writable text that creates an infrastructure gap signed languages are full human languages but they have often lacked equally mature written pathways that missing layer has consequences it limits not only literacy practice but also comparison reuse corpus building experimental design documentation and long term technical development why video is not the same as writing video is essential it preserves performance it captures timing movement and expression it is indispensable for documentation teaching and archive work but video is much weaker than writable text for editing close comparison searching annotation routine revision structured reuse everyday literacy practices put simply video lets you encounter language again writing lets you work with language as text this does not make video inferior in every respect it means that video and writing do different jobs media skill and literacy are not the same category of development learning to record or edit signed language may improve documentation and communication but it does not by itself create a durable read write symbolic pathway comparable to literacy research transcription and everyday writing some sign language notation and transcription systems are valuable for linguistic description and analytic work they help researchers label compare and study signed language but many were not designed to function as everyday writing systems that signers themselves would read and write directly in routine use so the issue is not whether transcription is useful the issue is whether a system supports native facing reading native facing writing durable text production structured data what signwriting adds sutton signwriting is a standardized system for writing signed languages this is the missing layer in the argument its importance is not that it replaces video or replaces every other notation system its importance is that it can add a durable written layer it provides readable signed language text writable signed language text direct representation of handshape movement facial expression and spatial organization structured and searchable text shareable and revisable written forms reusable representations for corpora annotation and computation the strongest documented empirical anchor for that claim is brazil where sustained signwriting culture shows that a community can build durable written pathways at scale within real educational and institutional use it is not a complete census or a global default but it is the clearest existing base for feasibility adoption and next phase study design the strongest formulation is not signwriting solves signed language education in general it is signwriting provides a plausible and already used route toward durable written pathways for signed language from writing system to infrastructure once a language has durable written pathways new forms of work become much more feasible it can support corpora lexicons datasets annotation systems educational materials computational pipelines repeatable research protocols without writable text these activities remain more fragmented more labor intensive and more dependent on time based media with writable text they begin to cohere into infrastructure that is why the argument does not end with a writing system it points toward technical stewardship standards work shared tools research integration long term institutional support what can be claimed rigorously the strongest claims are human language is not reducible to sound and can be reached through different embodied modalities sign languages are full natural languages literacy does not require sound and does not require vision alone signed languages benefit from durable written pathways just as spoken languages and braille users do written sign systems can support editing comparison annotation and structured language work in ways video alone cannot signwriting is already a real internationally used technical and scholarly system the weaker claims are broad population scale claims such as signwriting is already proven superior for literacy outcomes at large scale one written sign system has already solved signed language education in general the disciplined claim is the literature strongly supports feasibility use and scientific relevance more than it supports sweeping causal claims about large scale educational outcomes evidence and open questions the current evidence supports feasibility and sustained use more than it supports population scale outcome superiority evidence we can point to now brazil as the strongest documented empirical center for institutional use teacher training curriculum integration and sign language writing culture localized studies showing motivation comfort and literacy related gains in specific settings technical feasibility demonstrated through formal encoding fonts and usable reading and writing workflows open questions that still require stronger evidence updated national accounting beyond early self reported counts comparative longitudinal studies against strong bilingual video baselines replication across additional communities with independent outcome measures these are also the success criteria for the next research phase implication if language is not reducible to sound and if literacy depends on durable readable and writable systems then signed languages require written pathways in order to participate more fully in education research and technology signwriting provides such a pathway language does not depend on sound literacy does not depend on speech or vision but literacy does depend on having a writable system spoken languages have writing blind readers have braille signed languages have full linguistic structure but have often lacked equally mature written pathways signwriting addresses that gap the next step is not to keep arguing that signed languages can be written it is to build and sustain the infrastructure that allows them to function as written languages at scale qed steve slevinski

records.searchText: other sign language scripts and their conceptions essay essay for other sign language scripts and their conceptions a concise infrastructure facing entry naming a few prominent sign language script conceptions without turning them into the center of the signwriting case asl phabet hamnosys aslwrite and what they imply about written sign language series sutton signwriting infrastructure initiative other sign language scripts and their conceptions essay sutton signwriting infrastructure initiative other sign language scripts and their conceptions essay other sign language scripts and their conceptions other sign language scripts and their conceptions asl phabet hamnosys aslwrite and what they imply about written sign language a small number of other sign language script conceptions should be acknowledged because a serious signwriting initiative should acknowledge the visible landscape around it the point is not to produce a laundry list and not to turn every other system into an opponent the point is simpler the field is not empty different systems embody different conceptions of what signed writing is for signwriting remains strongest when presented as a constructive path forward rather than a reactive argument against competitors these systems also emerged from different communities and different practical needs some were built around educational goals some were built for linguistic notation some were built around alternate script design and handwriting interests signwriting has increasingly had to operate across all of those pressures while also becoming technical infrastructure why name other scripts at all signwriting should not be framed as if no one else has tried to represent signed language in written form a few systems are visible enough to deserve acknowledgment asl phabet hamnosys aslwrite each one matters for a different reason together they show that signed writing is an active intellectual and practical field rather than a private concern different conceptions of signed writing the most useful distinction is not merely graphic it is functional different systems answer different questions is signed writing mainly an alphabetic teaching device is it mainly analytic notation for research and transcription is it a plane based script meant to be read and written directly is it part of a larger infrastructure of standards software datasets and public continuity those are not identical goals the systems below should be understood in relation to those goals they should also not be collapsed into a single category merely because they all concern signed language institutional involvement funding and vision the systems also differ in where they were built how they were supported and what kind of future they were trying to create asl phabet institutional involvement tied to work around the arizona charter school and its formal affiliation with the university of arizona support structure educational and academic collaboration rather than a broad public infrastructure model vision establish an alphabetic principle for asl support signed word reading and help bridge toward written english this is a specific educational vision it is not the same as building an enduring public writing ecosystem for signed language across software standards and long term technical continuity hamnosys institutional involvement strongly academic in origin and long associated with the university of hamburg support structure scholarly and research oriented rather than community handwriting or public script adoption vision provide a detailed internationally applicable notation system for representing sign forms across languages that makes hamnosys significant it also makes it a different kind of project from signwriting aslwrite institutional involvement outside formal academic infrastructure support structure community driven and socially sustained vision offer an alternate plane based script with room for handwriting everyday use and a different visual culture of signed writing that community independence is part of what makes aslwrite interesting it is not trying to be a university notation system signwriting institutional involvement historically broader in use than in formal academic housing support structure international public use independent stewardship and growing need for durable institutional support vision function as a real writing system for signed languages while also supporting literacy software standards corpus work and long term infrastructure this is why signwriting raises a different scale of question it is not only a script design question it is also a stewardship and infrastructure question asl phabet asl phabet is the most visible linear alphabetic conception in current us discourse its importance here is not that it defines the future of signed writing its importance is that it is visible enough in us educational and academic settings to warrant direct acknowledgment the key point is it treats signed writing through a linear alphabetic conception it is tied to literacy oriented claims it has often been framed around individual word representation and instructional use it is part of the current us conversation whether or not one agrees with its design that makes it part of the surrounding landscape it does not have to become the center of the signwriting case hamnosys hamnosys is one of the best known names in sign language notation it represents a different conception from ordinary writable script its strength is analytic representation that matters for scholarly transcription representational precision comparative notation work it is best understood as part of a linguistic notation tradition but it is not the same thing as a broad native facing writing system intended for ordinary reading writing educational use and public literacy infrastructure that distinction should be made clearly and without hostility aslwrite aslwrite deserves acknowledgment as a second plane based script that matters because it shows signwriting is not the only serious attempt to treat signed writing spatially rather than only linearly aslwrite is also valuable because it keeps the question of handwriting alive it is useful to understand aslwrite as a community driven alternate spatial script design rather than merely as a technical curiosity its encoding approach is especially noteworthy the visible script is plane based some sign structure can be represented through an early linear encoded structure the current path appears partial and specialized rather than a mature public text workflow no broadly usable editor or implementation ecosystem is yet visible this is a real script conception not merely notation it is also a reminder that handwriting legibility and script culture remain live questions for signed writing what signwriting adds signwriting remains distinctive because it is more than a script idea in isolation it should also be described plainly as a formalized writing system not merely as illustrations of signs it already exists as a plane based writing system for natural signed language a public computational model through formal signwriting with fsw and swu as canonical encodings a software and standards ecosystem a dataset and continuity problem worth institutional support a public technical record large enough to require stewardship it also has its own handwriting tradition that tradition includes shorthand cursive block printing these traditions have appeared in pockets rather than through uniform large scale adoption that should not be mistaken for failure it more likely reflects the historical fact that signwriting s standardization and technical spread have outpaced the conditions needed for widespread handwriting culture that balance can change in the future where the sgnw code fits sgnw is the iso 15924 script code for signwriting in language tag terms a tag such as ase sgnw means ase american sign language sgnw written in the signwriting script that tag identifies language plus script it does not choose the concrete encoding used to carry the text for the technical stack described in this project formal signwriting is the public computable model for sutton signwriting fsw and swu are the two canonical encodings of that model the encoding choice should be named in the surrounding format schema protocol or profile rather than folded into the script subtag this distinction matters because sgnw should remain a script level identifier if another party devises another way to serialize sutton signwriting that would still be a question of encoding or format it would not require a different script code the narrower and stronger claim for this project is the standards facing technical stack for sutton signwriting is formal signwriting realized in fsw and swu while sgnw remains the script code used in language tags why this belongs in the infrastructure case acknowledging these scripts makes several things easier to say clearly first it shows that signwriting exists in a real field rather than in isolation second it prevents the infrastructure case from sounding naive or uninformed third it allows the initiative to remain constructive the central claim is not every other script must fail before signwriting can matter the stronger claim is signwriting is already the most complete path forward for durable writing computational encoding standards stewardship software and long term public infrastructure it is unusually complete because it spans script design handwriting computational encoding standards software dataset growth continuity obligations that is the lane this initiative should build bottom line other sign language scripts should be acknowledged briefly and fairly they help clarify the field they do not need to control the agenda the main job remains the same build signwriting as the strongest durable path forward for written signed language technical continuity research support and long term public infrastructure standards references iso 15924 code list iana language subtag registry rfc 5646 bcp 47 language tags

without sound research program for literacy without sound what could be studied next why brazil matters and what support would make it possible the science framing already does important work it establishes that language is not tied to sound that literacy is a durable symbolic expansion rather than a replacement for language and that signed writing deserves serious scientific attention but if the argument stops there it risks remaining permanently conceptual the stronger next move is a concrete signwriting centered research program this does not need to become a script comparison contest it also does not need to begin with large and expensive neuroscience promises the more useful question is what practical research program can begin now with real populations real materials and a real data and support plan why a concrete program is worth doing a more concrete program would help in several ways it would move the conversation from possibility to evidence it would make the support case easier for institutions and funders to understand it would connect the science series to a buildable research agenda it would let brazil function as a real empirical base rather than only as background evidence it would produce reusable materials datasets and study designs that later work can build on that last point matters most the best scientific program is not only one that produces findings it is one that produces reusable capacity what this program is not the most useful boundaries are as important as the ambitions this program is not a generic comparison of all sign language scripts a project organized around defeating asl phabet or any other system an attempt to settle every literacy question immediately a promise that neuroscience will answer everything first the better lane is narrower and more constructive build evidence for signwriting as readable and writable text study how durable signed text is used connect those studies to real educational and institutional contexts build the infrastructure that lets the science accumulate three research lanes the cleanest structure is a three lane program reading and literacy lane this lane asks what happens when signed language gains durable readable and writable text the central questions include how signwriting readers experience comfort and comprehension how written sign supports annotation revisiting and revision how text use differs from relying on video alone what kinds of written materials are easiest or hardest to use this lane stays close to the core claim of language without sound literacy changes what language users can do brazil as empirical center this lane uses brazil as the strongest current real world base brazil matters because it already offers documented institutional density coordinates if these terms stay clear the spatial side of the model stays clear the signbox is a spatial arrangement formal signwriting is not only a string format the signbox is where writing symbols become an arranged written object it matters because the system has to preserve visible composition while still working as text in software symbols formal signwriting depends on defined symbol classes and symbol ranges this is not only a matter of cataloging different symbol classes play different technical roles in writing centering searching and rendering at a glance class fsw range why it of sign text later layers depend on those distinctions search rendering centering and filtering all become easier to define when symbol classes are explicit implementations often expose these uses coordinates to preserve spatial relation these coordinates are not incidental metadata they are part of how the written form becomes usable in software for example the signbox portion of this sign contains m525x535 as the signbox marker and signbox coordinate four writing symbols each with its own coordinate that is how visible arrangement becomes text software can work with at a finer level each placed symbol follows the same pattern this can be read as s2e748 the symbol key 483x510 the top left coordinate of that symbol image inside the signbox the repeated pairing of symbol key plus coordinate is what lets the signbox function as a structured spatial object rather than a loose visual impression bounding boxes and maximum coordinates bounding box logic matters because written signs do not encode symbol size directly formal signwriting therefore needs a clear account of symbol bounding boxes sign bounding boxes maximum coordinate calculations those calculations support layout sizing and downstream rendering behavior the basic logic is each symbol has its own measured width and height each placed symbol starts with a symbol key and a top left coordinate preprocessing temporarily expands each symbol into a tight bounding box with top left and bottom right coordinates the sign bounding box is derived from the extreme left right top and bottom extents across all placed symbols maximum coordinate calculations make the full spatial footprint explicit that is why a sign can be laid out centered printed and rendered without abandoning its textual form in other words the sign remains text the text carries its spatial arrangement and that arrangement can be computed consistently centering and normalization centering is one of the most important technical moves in the system it simplifies layout and supports more consistent spatial handling across tools it also improves comparison between implementations stable sign display in reusable interfaces downstream search and rendering workflows practical interchange across software generations the centering logic should be stated more concretely horizontal centering first looks for hcenter symbols vertical centering first looks for vcenter symbols if those ranges are absent centering falls back to all placed symbols the midpoint is calculated from the selected minimum and maximum extents all symbol coordinates are shifted together the resulting maximum x and y values become part of the preprocessing result the temporary bottom right coordinates are then discarded leaving normalized sign text the important point is that normalization requires symbol size knowledge that is not stored directly in the written sign the sign remains text but its spatial layout has to be computed during preprocessing the conceptual point is then clear the sign must be normalized strongly enough to support stable technical use core point without this spatial layer formal signwriting risks being misunderstood as only a string format with it the model becomes visibly coherent and usable in software
