modern literacy is taught through line based text letters proceed one after another words proceed one after another sentences proceed one after another that familiarity is real it is not the definition of writing writing can use space in different ways that matters for how writing is read written edited encoded searched and shared it also matters for how signwriting should be understood the core claim is simple writing is not inherently linear once that is accepted a second claim becomes easier to see signed language does not need to be forced into a line in order to be written writing and space one useful way to think about writing is to ask how it uses space three broad tendencies help make that visible point structure is concentrated into a bounded written unit line meaning unfolds mainly through ordered sequence plane meaning depends on spatial composition across two dimensions these are not rigid boxes they are a simple lens many writing systems show more than one tendency at once but the distinction is still useful because it loosens a mistaken assumption writing does not come in only one shape point point like writing compresses structure into a bounded unit the unit may still contain internal parts those parts may matter for lookup learning analysis or history but readers still approach the written form as an integrated whole chinese characters are a useful example at the unit level that does not mean chinese writing is non sequential at every level characters still occur in strings phrases and sentences the narrower point is this a written unit can function as a whole and still be decomposed for sorting indexing and software handling this matters because it separates two ideas that are often confused integrated writing does not mean unanalyzable writing decomposition for technical purposes does not make the writing fundamentally linear line line based writing is the familiar modern case meaning unfolds through sequence order matters adjacency matters parsing depends heavily on progression alphabetic writing abjads abugidas and many other systems are line dominant in this sense in these systems the writer mainly provides a sequence typography shaping and layout often handle much of the final rendering ligatures combining marks stacked signs and cursive joining may create visual complexity but the dominant model remains writing is authored as a sequence and rendered as a line that contrast matters because it makes the next step easier to see in line based systems the writer mainly provides a sequence in signwriting the writer composes spatial relations as part of the writing itself plane plane based writing uses space directly position matters relation matters grouping matters sometimes simultaneity matters the writer does not merely choose symbols in order the writer composes them spatially this is where signwriting becomes especially important a written sign is not only a list of handshapes movements contacts orientations facial markers and locations it is a spatial composition in which those relations are part of the writing itself that is why the clearest statement is plane writing makes spatial relation part of authorship this does not mean every visual notation counts as a writing system it does mean that signwriting should be understood as a genuine case of writing that works through the plane rather than through a line alone signwriting as plane based writing once the default assumption of linearity is removed signwriting becomes much easier to see clearly it can be understood as a writable way of composing signed language in space the written sign is not authored mainly as a sequence and then finished by layout it is authored through symbol choice and spatial relation from the start that matters because signed language is already spatial in structure and expression if a writing system for signed language exists it does not need to imitate the shape of spoken print in order to be real it needs to be adequate to the language it writes that is what makes plane based writing so important here the alphabet is part of the writing plane based writing is not free placement alone it also depends on a stable symbol inventory for modern sutton signwriting infrastructure that shared inventory is iswa 2010 this matters not only as a catalog of symbols but as a structured reference layer behind writing editors fonts websites datasets encoding its organization is disciplined 652 panels of symbols each panel arranged in a 6 by 16 grid stable distinctions for handshape palm facing fill rotation and related contrasts those distinctions are part of the writing system they are not mere display preferences if tools fonts or websites silently redefine them the result is not harmless variation the result is fractured infrastructure this is why the alphabet belongs inside the argument about the shape of writing the plane depends on a stable alphabet not only on free placement human writing and machine encoding at this point one distinction becomes essential human writing and machine encoding are not the same layer human writing this concerns what writers compose what readers perceive which spatial relations matter which symbols belong to the writing itself for signwriting this layer is planar machine encoding this concerns storage transmission search sorting parsing computation a writing system can be plane based for readers and writers while still needing a serialized form for software that is not a contradiction it is a design problem the important point is serialization for software does not prove that the writing was really linear all along why this matters for signed language signed languages are full human languages the pathway model from language without sound applies here language pathways are complete without writing while literacy adds a durable symbolic pathway that changes what can be read written revised and shared but they have often lacked equally durable written pathways instead they have often been pushed toward video gloss researcher facing transcription line based tools that do not match the spatial shape of the language those tools are useful they are not the same thing as writable text video preserves performance gloss supports cross language reference transcription supports analysis but none of them automatically create everyday readable and writable text within the language itself that is the infrastructure gap signed languages have full linguistic structure but they have often lacked equally durable writable pathways that gap affects literacy editing comparison lexicons corpora annotation search datasets long term infrastructure comparative cases the differences become easier to see through a few simple comparisons hearing child plus print literacy a hearing child usually develops spoken language first and then line based literacy through print the written pathway is sequential text deaf child plus signwriting a deaf child may develop signed language first and then potentially gain literacy through a writable plane based system the written pathway is spatial text spatial specialist notation mathematics music chemistry and other specialist notations already show that literate readers can work with systems in which placement grouping and layout carry meaning these are not identical to full natural language writing but they show that literacy does not have to be purely linear video or transcription of sign video and transcription are valuable they preserve language for documentation and research but they do not by themselves provide the same kind of everyday readable and writable text that a writing system provides what follows once this is understood if signwriting is real plane based writing then several consequences follow it needs a stable alphabet stable symbol identity compatible tools processable encoding shared editors fonts and websites corpora datasets and research support it also needs an encoding model that preserves authored composition rather than trying to replace it with derived rendering in other words the question is no longer whether signed language could be written the question becomes what infrastructure is required once signed language has writable text of its own that is why this argument matters it is not only a theory of writing it is also a practical claim about literacy tools compatibility and research support evidence and open questions current evidence is strongest on feasibility and use and weaker on comparative outcome scale evidence we can point to now brazil as the strongest documented empirical center for plane based sign language writing in practice localized instructional programs that show durable use within real classrooms and institutions technical feasibility demonstrated through encoding fonts and usable writing workflows open questions that still require stronger evidence updated national accounting beyond early self reported counts longitudinal comparisons against strong bilingual video baselines replication across additional communities with independent outcome measures these are also the success criteria for the next research phase conclusion writing does not come in only one shape some writing compresses structure into bounded units some unfolds through sequence some composes meaning in space signwriting belongs in that third case strongly enough to matter once that is seen signed language no longer has to be treated as if writing for it must imitate the line in order to count readable and writable plane based text becomes thinkable the strongest documented empirical anchor for that claim is brazil where a sustained signwriting culture shows plane based writing in real educational and institutional use it does not resolve all scale questions but it provides the clearest current base for feasibility and next phase study design rather than a global default visible composition can remain with the writer and processable encoding can support that writing without redefining it and once that becomes thinkable a whole new layer of literacy tooling compatibility datasets and research becomes possible qed steve slevinski

records.contentExcerpt: Unicode Overview Current position and why the issue keeps returning The current official Unicode SignWriting encoding is not enough for the Sutton SignWriting production ecosystem. That is not because Unicode has no SignWriting symbols. It is because the official model does not p...
records.searchText: unicode overview essay essay for unicode overview short baseline explanation of why character naming stable symbols and written signs remain different questions for signwriting current position and why the issue keeps returning series unicode and signwriting unicode overview essay unicode and signwriting unicode overview essay unicode overview unicode overview current position and why the issue keeps returning the current official unicode signwriting encoding is not enough for the sutton signwriting production ecosystem that is not because unicode has no signwriting symbols it is because the official model does not preserve stable symbol identity cleanly across the full system and it does not provide a compatible model for written signs current position the sutton signwriting resources currently hold that the official unicode 8 signwriting encoding is not fully compatible with the established sutton signwriting production ecosystem the official model does not preserve stable symbol identity cleanly across the full iswa layer the main unresolved issue is spatial composition sorting and collation are also not adequate production ready tools fonts and datasets cannot simply be moved to the official encoding without loss or redesign fsw remains the main production encoding in practice swu remains the unicode oriented isomorphic representation supported by the sutton signwriting resources the existing sutton signwriting ecosystem already carries too much real data software publishing and international work to accept a nominal standard that breaks the ecosystem it claims to support why this issue keeps returning the issue keeps returning because many people reasonably assume that once unicode encodes a script the script problem is solved that assumption does not hold here unicode encoded a signwriting block but it did not preserve the full symbol model cleanly and it did not produce a compatible practical model for written signs there is a difference between naming characters preserving writer selected symbols and encoding complete written signs that difference is why the issue keeps returning in standards conversations developer questions wikimedia support discussions package and library design public assumptions about whether the signwriting problem has already been solved it also returns because the official unicode path became visible enough to shape public expectations even though the deeper writing system issues remained unresolved what the official encoding does cover the official unicode signwriting block covers base characters fill modifiers rotation modifiers that means it can name much of the symbol inventory it does not mean that the full symbol model remains stable and writer selected across the whole system it does not by itself provide a compatible solution for full stable symbol identity across the facial system full written sign representation stable spatial composition direct compatibility with the existing sutton signwriting datasets clean migration of production fonts and tools straightforward sorting and collation in the facial area the official diacritic model introduces a deeper break instead of simply preserving the writer s selected facial symbols and their authored placement the model asks the writer for a sequence that the font then interprets and arranges into a rendered face that pushes part of symbol formation into font behavior and designer interpretation it also means that the official model does not merely fall short at the written sign level it also destabilizes symbol identity at part of the symbol level it also leaves a more basic question unresolved how a written signwriting word should be encoded so that it remains faithful to the writing system and usable in software that question is where the gap between symbol encoding and writing support becomes most obvious the practical difference the difference can be stated simply official unicode signwriting names much of the symbol block but does not preserve the full symbol model cleanly formal signwriting names and structures written signs the writing system in practice needs more than a symbol block it needs a stable way to represent sequence when sequence is written spatial composition when composition is written interchange with existing datasets search sorting rendering and software processing that is why the real disagreement is not best framed as unicode versus no unicode the better framing is naming characters without fully preserving writer selected symbols versus encoding complete written signs in a usable compatible way that framing is more honest and more useful than treating the issue as personal resistance to unicode itself why fsw and swu still matter fsw is still the true production encoding of the sutton signwriting resources it is ascii durable practical and independent of the limits of official unicode signwriting swu is the unicode oriented isomorphic representation used by the sutton signwriting resources it makes the two part word highly visible in one dimensional text a sequence of symbols followed by symbols with coordinates that representation is currently usable not only for interchange and inspection but also for ai and dataset work when enough data is available this is also why swu should not be confused with official unicode signwriting they are not the same design that distinction should stay explicit whenever a reader sees the word unicode and assumes all unicode oriented signwriting work is one thing why the disagreement is not only about fonts it is common to ask whether this is mainly a rendering problem it is not better fonts alone do not solve the facial diacritic model where font behavior mediates part of symbol formation the lack of a workable spatial composition model in official unicode signwriting incompatibility with existing encoded datasets sorting and collation problems required overrides and custom behavior in tools the gap between a symbol inventory and real written word workflows the distinction matters because a very good font can make an incomplete model look more complete than it really is that is part of why later public visibility increased confusion instead of reducing it this is also why the question cannot be reduced to unicode solved symbols and only writing remains at least in the facial system the official model does not preserve symbol identity in a way that remains cleanly compatible with formal signwriting the scale of the practical ecosystem the sutton signwriting ecosystem already includes real public websites real software tools real fonts real dictionaries real published materials real corpora with more than one million written signs across dozens of sign languages those assets are the standard by which any unicode path should be judged the question is not whether another design can be imagined the question is whether it can carry real writing real tools and real data without breaking them the ecosystem should be judged as a real production environment not as a thought experiment waiting for its first serious implementation what would count as progress progress with unicode would not require immediate agreement on everything it would require acknowledgment that the current official unicode signwriting encoding is not sufficient for compatible production use acknowledgment that the facial diacritic model does not preserve stable writer selected symbol identity cleanly acknowledgment that spatial composition remains unresolved honest technical discussion of collation compatibility and migration a concrete path for renewed discussion with the support of a voting member that is the minimum honest starting point

records.contentExcerpt: UTC Technical Note Draft note on compatibility, symbol identity, spatial composition, and next steps The Sutton SignWriting Resources regard the current official Unicode SignWriting encoding as insufficient for compatible production use within the established Sutton SignWriting e...
records.searchText: utc technical note essay essay for utc technical note a formal draft note stating that official unicode signwriting can name much of the character set but still does not preserve stable symbol identity or provide compatible written sign support draft note on compatibility symbol identity spatial composition and next steps series unicode and signwriting utc technical note essay unicode and signwriting utc technical note essay utc technical note utc technical note draft note on compatibility symbol identity spatial composition and next steps the sutton signwriting resources regard the current official unicode signwriting encoding as insufficient for compatible production use within the established sutton signwriting ecosystem summary the current official unicode signwriting encoding does not fully solve symbols cleanly and it does not solve written signs the core distinction is this naming characters is not the same as preserving stable symbol identity preserving stable symbol identity is not the same as encoding complete written signs the current official encoding can name much of the signwriting character set it does not provide a workable compatible solution for stable writer selected symbol identity across the full system written signs as they exist in current production use spatial composition sorting and collation aligned with current practice migration from existing data fonts and tools without redesign in the facial system specifically the official model does not simply preserve the writer s choice of facial symbols and their authored placement in the signbox instead the writer enters a sequence of facial elements and the font takes on part of the job of arranging them into a rendered face that means symbol formation is partly mediated by font behavior and designer interpretation rather than being fixed by a shared writer selected symbol inventory this breaks compatibility at the symbol layer before the larger written sign problem is even fully considered renewed technical review is needed rather than continued assumption that migration to the current official encoding is the expected path terms sutton signwriting the writing system and broader technical ecosystem fsw formal signwriting in ascii the main production encoding in practice swu signwriting in unicode the unicode oriented isomorphic representation supported by the sutton signwriting resources official unicode signwriting the current signwriting encoding standardized in unicode 8 and later versions in some internal discussions the official unicode encoding is informally called uni8 to distinguish it from swu inside the current ecosystem that informal term helps prevent confusion between official unicode signwriting swu as supported by the sutton signwriting resources current problem the current problem is not whether unicode can name signwriting characters it can the current problem is whether the official unicode signwriting encoding can carry the established sutton signwriting production ecosystem without loss redesign or incompatible workarounds at present it cannot this is an interoperability judgment about what the current official encoding can carry in practice not about the sincerity of past participants character naming is not enough the official unicode signwriting block covers base characters fill modifiers rotation modifiers that means it can name much of the character set that does not mean the full symbol model remains stable and writer selected across the whole system the facial system has a closed shared inventory comparable to iswa symbol selection written signs can be encoded in a compatible practical way the standards question cannot stop at character naming it also has to ask whether symbol identity remains stable whether writers still select symbols directly whether written words can be encoded without breaking the writing system symbol identity breaks in the facial system in the facial system the official model does not preserve direct writer control over a stable set of selected facial symbols and their authored placement instead the writer enters a sequence of facial elements that the font interprets and arranges into a rendered face that shifts part of symbol formation into font behavior and designer interpretation it also means the font can end up deciding more than simple drawing details it can affect how facial elements are stacked grouped normalized or spatially resolved even though those choices can matter to the writing itself this is not a small rendering detail it means the official model does not merely encode a symbol inventory imperfectly it changes how final facial symbols come into being there is no closed master list of facial diacritic combinations that functions as a shared exhaustive inventory in the same way as iswa symbol selection as a result font implementations are pushed toward copying a previous interpretation or inventing one this is why the current official encoding should not be described as though it solved symbols cleanly and only left writing unresolved at least in the facial system it does not preserve stable symbol identity in a way that remains fully compatible with formal signwriting spatial composition remains unresolved the core writing system issue is spatial composition signwriting is not only a symbol inventory it is a writing system in which the visible written sign depends on spatial composition earlier framing around unicode signwriting described a two stage path first the symbol set then an encoding that takes symbols and turns them into signs the first stage advanced the second did not become a workable compatible standard the unresolved issue is not merely how to draw a symbol string it is how to encode a written sign in a way that remains faithful to the writing system processable in software compatible with current data this is the point where the standards question and the software question become the same question collation and sorting remain unresolved sorting and collation are not incidental details they affect dictionaries corpus order search behavior software workflows interoperability across tools the problem is not simply that a sort order has not yet been tuned the problem is that the current official model does not align cleanly with the established sutton signwriting production ecosystem and its practical needs two design choices matter directly here the facial diacritic model which moves part of symbol identity into font interpretation inherent first fill and first rotation values which weaken explicit symbol identity and complicate stable sorting if the facial diacritic model were removed and explicit first fill and first rotation values were restored instead of being inherent compatibility and sorting would move much closer to the formal signwriting model this is why collation should be treated as a signal of deeper incompatibility rather than as a small secondary defect compatibility with current production use the current sutton signwriting production ecosystem includes real tools real websites real fonts real corpora real dictionaries real published materials that ecosystem currently relies on fsw and swu the official unicode signwriting encoding does not currently provide a compatible migration path that preserves direct writer selected symbol identity across the full system existing data current production tooling practical search and sorting workflows stable written sign representation that should matter to utc because an encoding standard becomes much more valuable when it can serve as a stable interchange layer for real production use without that formal standardization risks naming a script without actually serving its living technical ecosystem why this is not only a font issue the issue is sometimes framed as though a sufficiently complete font would solve the problem that framing is too narrow fonts matter but the deeper issues remain stable writer selected facial symbols and authored placement in the facial system a shared symbol inventory that does not depend on font mediated composition representation of written signs not only characters compatibility with existing encoded data sorting and collation workflow and software integration the absence of a completed compatible spatial composition model strong fonts can hide structural gaps temporarily they cannot remove them practical position the practical position of the sutton signwriting resources is fsw remains the main production encoding swu remains the supported unicode oriented isomorphic representation the official unicode signwriting encoding is not currently used as the main production path that position is not based on refusal to engage unicode in principle it is based on incompatibility in practice it is also compatible with continued technical engagement if a serious path opens that path would need to begin by acknowledging that the current official model did not fully solve symbols cleanly and did not solve writing evidence from current use the real world ecosystem is now large enough that compatibility should be judged against production use not only theoretical possibility the current ecosystem includes more than one million written signs across dozens of sign languages together with real software public resources dictionaries and published materials in addition current country level cases such as brazil and south korea show that encoded signwriting is not hypothetical or merely local they also show why the standard of evaluation should be real writing and real infrastructure not only internal standards elegance requested next step the requested next step is modest and concrete acknowledgment that current official unicode signwriting is not sufficient for compatible production use acknowledgment that symbol identity is not preserved cleanly across the full system acknowledgment that the facial diacritic model mediates part of symbol selection and arrangement through font behavior acknowledgment that spatial composition remains unresolved renewed technical review of compatibility collation and migration a concrete path for follow up discussion with support from a voting member start with honest technical acknowledgment of the present gap closing the current official unicode signwriting encoding should not be treated as though it already solved the practical writing system problem it did not fully solve symbols cleanly it did not solve the compatible written sign problem in the facial system it displaced writer selected symbol identity with font mediated composition that distinction remains the central technical issue everything else depends on whether that distinction is acknowledged clearly

records.searchText: brazil as the empirical center of signwriting evidence notes evidence notes for brazil as the empirical center of signwriting series sutton signwriting infrastructure initiative brazil as the empirical center of signwriting evidence notes sutton signwriting infrastructure initiative brazil as the empirical center of signwriting evidence notes evidence notes brazil as the empirical center of signwriting evidence notes brazil as the empirical center of signwriting documented institutional density research opportunity and why brazil matters these evidence notes record the narrow factual basis for the brazil brief it does not attempt a complete national history of signwriting in brazil it does aim to show why brazil can already be treated as the strongest documented empirical center in the current public record the core documented baseline the most useful compact baseline currently available from the project record comes from the brazilian sign language wikipedia project page on meta wiki last edited in february 2018 that page states that replies had been received from 21 different schools in brazil those schools came from 13 brazilian states the total represented over 1 000 students all of those schools were described there as using signwriting in the classroom for present purposes the strongest use of that baseline is a documented floor of institutional density not a complete census of brazilian signwriting activity that formulation is strong enough to support infrastructure and research planning without overstating the evidence states listed in the 2018 accounting the 2018 project page listed the following states and counts state count in project accounting rio grande do sul 4 schools santa catarina 2 schools paran 1 school s o paulo 1 school rio de janeiro 1 school esp rito santo 1 school sergipe 1 school bahia 2 schools cear 3 schools or projects piau 1 school maranh o 2 schools acre 1 school amazonas 1 school this state spread is one reason the brazil case should not be treated as a purely local phenomenon examples named directly in the project page the same project page named a mix of university and school settings including the signwriting research group at the federal university of santa catarina in florian polis a deaf school using signwriting in santa maria the federal university of cear with a signwriting partnership involving a deaf school in fortaleza the federal university of maranh o in s o lu s the deaf school gere in ipia bahia these examples matter because they show that the documented ecosystem included university settings school settings regional spread training interest tied to real signwriting users why this baseline matters the 2018 accounting does not prove everything it does prove enough to support several stronger claims brazil is not merely an anecdotal success case brazil contains repeated institutional use rather than a single cluster brazil provides a realistic base for future literacy and reading studies brazil provides a realistic base for future corpus and infrastructure work this is why brazil should be treated as a major research center in planning language even before a more exhaustive national update is completed additional grounding from the signwriting org brazil archive the country specific brazil page on signwriting org strengthens the case further because it functions as a public aggregation page rather than a single isolated report that page links classroom activity signpuddle libras resources literature archives historical materials course materials ufsc letras libras references software and dictionary links early historical pages such as the 1996 1997 brazil history and signnet documentation this matters because it shows that the brazilian record is not reducible to one grant page or one school list it already has the shape of a country archive additional grounding from the symposium archive the symposium record strengthens the brazil case in a different way it shows repeated brazilian participation across years and across categories at the current public count this review identified 37 brazil related symposium presentations between 2014 and 2024 examples include historical and institutional framing signwriting in brazilian deaf education 1996 to present reading research aspects of reading and signwriting online instruction teaching and learning signwriting in an online course and magic behind the cams phonological and linguistic work the relevance of signwriting as a way of transcribing the phonology of sign languages and linguistic terminological productivity between libras and signwriting books and teaching materials escrita de sinais sem mist rios local school implementation and literacy ipia maranh o belo horizonte and bilingual curriculum presentations this repeated presence matters because it shows that brazil appears in the public signwriting record not only as a user base but also as a continuing source of reflection pedagogy software and research it also shows clear internal development early historical and educational consolidation in 2014 software and corpus extensions by 2015 2017 explicit literacy and curricular framing in 2017 2018 a much broader 2024 layer involving curriculum books literature reading comfort cultural identification and lexical analysis historically specific claims supported by the symposium record several historically specific claims in the public record are especially useful for future versions of this brief a 2014 symposium presentation stated that signwriting reached brazil in 1996 through the work of costa stumpf and borba the same presentation reported that the signnet project included funding from cnpq and protem and produced the sw edit software the same presentation reported that since 2006 signwriting had formed a 180 hour part of the letras libras curriculum at ufsc that presentation also reported that the first two ufsc distance offerings in 2006 and 2010 reached more than 1 200 deaf and hearing students from 17 brazilian states plus the federal district another 2014 presentation reported that the libras escrita publisher was founded in 2011 and that its 70 hour distance course had already reached more than 50 students from 16 brazilian states plus one student from portugal the ipia presentation reported signwriting based literacy work there since 2012 these claims are still best handled as cited historical reports rather than as contemporary verified census statements but taken together they greatly strengthen the argument that brazil already has unusual depth as a signwriting center for a fuller presentation by presentation review including counts by year and category plus one line review notes for each identified brazil presentation see symposium review md for a compact bibliography of the most useful brazilian anchors dissertations symposium items and technical works see key works md what still needs verification several broader claims are plausible but should still be handled carefully unless verified program by program these include claims about exactly which current degree pathways in libras include signwriting instruction which institutions continue the same level of use today how many current students are actively using signwriting rather than being merely exposed to it how many current research projects depend on signwriting directly those are worthwhile future tasks they are not prerequisites for treating the 2018 accounting as a strong documented floor why continuity risk is visible in brazil brazil is also one of the clearest places where infrastructure interruptions become socially visible because the brazilian signwriting community is large active and institutionally distributed outages are felt quickly rather than disappearing into private maintenance work project notes from a prior legacy server crash indicate that people in brazil were actively discussing continuity and support while the service was down that does not change the main empirical center claim of this brief it strengthens the infrastructure reading of it brazil is not only a dense use case it is also a place where continuity failures are immediately experienced by real users why english language synthesis still matters brazil s role in signwriting is larger than this one project page it also includes dissertations educational practice classroom use software and corpus work sustained regional and institutional participation but much of that record remains under read in english language conversations this makes translation synthesis and public research framing especially important selected references b zoli d m f 2021 educa o bil ngue de surdos o uso da escrita de sinais signwriting na aprendizagem do portugu s como segunda l ngua doctoral dissertation universidade federal de santa catarina meta wiki 2018 february 23 grants project slevinski brazilian sign language wikipedia project wikimedia foundation signwriting org n d signwriting in brazil escrita de sinais brasil signwriting org n d brazil uses signwriting 1996 1997 signwriting symposium 2014 signwriting in brazilian deaf education 1996 to present signwriting symposium 2014 teaching and learning signwriting in an online course experiences of the teacher and students signwriting symposium 2014 magic behind the cams teaching signwriting in an online course signwriting symposium 2014 the perspective of the deaf from ipia bahia brazil about signwriting signwriting symposium 2024 implementa o do signwriting em curr culos escolares bil ngues stumpf m r 2005 aprendizagem de escrita de l ngua de sinais pelo sistema signwriting l ngua de sinais no papel e no computador doctoral dissertation universidade federal do rio grande do sul bottom line an honest and complete history of signwriting in brazil would be extensive this brief does not try to replace that history it makes a narrower claim brazil already has enough documented institutional density to be treated as the strongest current empirical center of signwriting

The expectation described...
records.searchText: chronology and record historical notes historical notes for chronology and record series unicode and signwriting chronology and record historical notes unicode and signwriting chronology and record historical notes historical notes historical notes turning points behind the chronology the original expectation one of the most important historical points is that unicode discussion around sutton signwriting was not originally framed as if the symbol block alone would finish the work the expectation described in the signwriting materials was a two stage path first the symbol inventory then an encoding that takes symbols and turns them into signs keep that two stage framing in view and the later disagreement becomes much easier to understand the early compromise problem the earliest unicode proposals already reflect compromise pressure from the sutton signwriting point of view this matters because some of the structural material needed for written signs was reduced or removed in order to move the symbol proposal forward that helped the symbol stage advance it also helped create the later compatibility problem the 2012 break the 2012 proposal cycle matters because it sharpened the sense that the unicode path was no longer tracking the same design goals as the sutton signwriting production ecosystem in the sutton signwriting account this is where the gap became clearer sorting was compromised facial diacritic design changed layout remained unresolved compatibility confidence weakened this is also why martin hosken s later layout discussion note matters historically it shows that the problem was still open enough to require exploratory design the 2015 turning point when the sutton signwriting block was officially added to unicode 8 0 in 2015 many outside readers understandably inferred that signwriting had now been solved in unicode from the sutton signwriting perspective that was the beginning of a lasting public misunderstanding the symbol block had been approved that did not mean that stable symbol identity had been fully preserved across the system the compatible written sign problem had not been solved either that distinction is one of the main reasons the issue remains alive the post approval visibility problem once the official unicode encoding existed it began to influence public expectations open source work fonts library design wikimedia and related public knowledge systems this matters historically because visibility can create legitimacy even when compatibility is still unresolved that is part of why the unicode fork became noisy in public space the 2020 font moment google s noto sans signwriting was historically significant because it made the official unicode signwriting design more concrete for outsiders that mattered in two directions at once it showed the official design could be rendered in a serious public font it also increased the chance that readers would assume the deeper writing system problem was therefore solved historically this is one of the moments where the appearance of progress risked masking continuing incompatibility the 2026 reopening wikimedia s renewed outreach in 2026 marks another turning point it suggests that the unicode issue may become live again in a more practical way if a voting member is willing to engage the issue is no longer only about what happened it is also about whether the next discussion will begin from the real history or from a flattened public story

technical notes technical notes formal signwriting technical notes formal signwriting the two part word sign text and the core model these technical notes collect a few compact details behind the core model minimal example one compact example is enough to show the architecture the structure is a for the temporal prefix m525x535 for the spatial signbox that is the whole architecture in miniature the rest of the model simply makes the consequences explicit i minimal definitions the opening layer depends on a few stable definitions term definition sign a required signbox with an optional temporal prefix temporal prefix the optional sequence preserving part of the word spatial signbox the required composition preserving part of the word fsw and swu plain text encodings of the same model with fsw commonly canonical in processing and swu supported for unicode oriented interchange style string optional markup for presentation and customization these definitions should remain stable even as later appendices and tools become more detailed ii validity and sequence the validity boundary should stay explicit a full sign requires a signbox a sequence only string is only a fragment a signbox only string is a valid sign sequence sensitive sorting is unavailable when no temporal prefix is present the sequence should also be described carefully it is written by an author it is created according to a theory of sorting or analysis it is not derivative markup recoverable automatically from the signbox alone val s sign spelling guidelines is the most prevalent current theory formal signwriting stays theory agnostic even though mixed theories reduce the value of shared sorting iii technical position the technical position is formal signwriting is a stable technical model for written sign text the visible writing and the computational representation are related but distinct layers fsw and swu are plain text encodings of the same model with fsw commonly canonical in processing and swu supported for unicode oriented interchange the style string is not canonical sign text and should be treated as optional markup for presentation the two part word is the core explanatory move search sorting rendering and transformation are consequences of that move iv why the two part word belongs first the two part word is not only a definitional convenience it is the move that lets the rest of the architecture stay coherent encodings can preserve the model grammar can describe the model search can operate over the model rendering can realize the model implementation can maintain the model across software generations without this opening move later layers risk feeling like a loose collection of mechanisms v minimal example broken apart the worked sign can be broken apart this way the first line preserves theory based sequence data the second line preserves spatial composition that is enough to show why later layers can treat the same sign as text geometry grammar search target rendering source vi consequences of the opening move once the two part word is accepted several consequences follow immediately sequence sensitive handling becomes possible sequence theory can vary without changing the formal model spatial placement remains visible and usable in software validity can be defined formally search can operate on structured text rendering can remain downstream from encoding implementation can preserve the model across tools and workflows vii small core growing perimeter the core of the model should stay small the two part word canonical encodings spatial composition valid structure downstream rendering and styling boundaries around that core the surrounding record can continue to grow new tooling new datasets reference appendices compatibility notes implementation reports that balance keeps the model readable while letting the reference material expand

records.searchText: fsw and swu technical notes technical notes for fsw and swu series formal signwriting fsw and swu technical notes formal signwriting fsw and swu technical notes technical notes fsw and swu technical notes fsw and swu plain text encodings character sets and conversion these technical notes collect compact notes behind the encoding layer quick examples minimal conversion examples i canonical encodings fsw and swu are core encodings of formal signwriting text they belong to the technical model rather than to an auxiliary display layer but their operational roles can still differ for stewardship and implementation work fsw is commonly the canonical internal form for processing storage and indexing while swu is supported for conversion input output boundaries and unicode oriented workflows ii minimal inventory the shared inventory can be summarized compactly description fsw swu sequence marker a u 1d800 signbox markers b l m r u 1d801 to u 1d804 numbers 250 to 749 u 1d80c to u 1d9ff null symbol s00000 u 40000 symbols s10000 to s38b07 u 40001 to u 4f428 in swu this means five structural markers from u 1d800 to u 1d804 500 dedicated number characters from u 1d80c to u 1d9ff the null symbol at u 40000 62 504 symbol characters from u 40001 to u 4f428 iii symbol key structure fsw symbol keys deserve a more explicit reading for a key such as s10000 s marks a symbol key 100 is the three hex digit base symbol 0 is the fill value 0 is the rotation value in regex terms the pattern is the base is especially important because symbol typing is based on the base symbol not on fill or rotation that is why range tests for writing movement punctuation hcenter or vcenter all operate on the base value the swu symbol mapping is equally systematic and is best stated in two steps for s10000 that produces id 1 u 40001 iv script code and language tags sgnw is the iso 15924 script code for signwriting a tag such as ase sgnw identifies american sign language written in the signwriting script that identifies language plus script not whether the text is serialized in fsw or swu for public technical guidance the encoding choice should be stated separately by the format schema protocol or profile v separation of concerns encoding should stay distinct from grammar search rendering styling vi style string distinction the style string should not be taught as part of the plain text encoding itself it is better understood as optional light markup for presentation and customization that makes it analogous to a rendering adjunct rather than the text proper vii conversion as continuity conversion between fsw and swu is part of the continuity of the model it shows that formal signwriting is not tied to one single character strategy while still remaining a coherent technical system at the symbol level conversion remains systematic rather than ad hoc base symbol identity is preserved fill is preserved rotation is preserved viii coordinate and number range note the shared numeric restriction matters enough to state explicitly valid values run from 250 to 749 fsw writes them with decimal digits swu writes them with number characters from u 1d80c to u 1d9ff the swu number mapping is direct so an swu coordinate is two consecutive number characters one for x and one for y with no literal x separator for example this preserves the same coordinate model across both encodings that is why conversion belongs to the encoding layer and not to rendering ix recurring confusions to avoid three confusions recur often confusing encoding with rendering confusing text with styling confusing character choice with model choice confusing base symbol with full symbol key confusing script code with encoding name those confusions create downstream trouble in software teaching and technical writing x appendix note on plain text the important point is not only that fsw is ascii and swu is unicode the important point is that both are still plain text even when one is commonly used as the canonical internal form and the other is preferred for some unicode facing workflows that is why they can support storage parsing conversion comparison search before any rendering decision is made

be matched filtered and located those are closely connected technical questions but they should not be collapsed into one artifact iii order of technical dependence the layer order is encodings first spatial model next grammar after that search as a downstream use of the grammar iv transformations remain cross cutting they belong naturally in multiple places encoding conversion grammar decomposition query construction rendering pipelines implementation workflows v what the grammar has to establish the grammar has to establish a conceptual explanation of well formed text an operational account of token patterns a clear relation between the two part word and validation enough rigor that later software work does not need to reinvent the layer vi appendix note on token roles the grammar is easier to teach when token roles are kept explicit structural markers say where major parts begin symbol keys identify symbols coordinates place symbols spatially combination rules define what counts as a full sign that keeps the grammar understandable before it becomes fully formal

sutton signwriting infrastructure initiative infrastructure and stewardship snapshot snapshot infrastructure and stewardship snapshot companion snapshot for infrastructure and stewardship snapshot purpose this one page snapshot is designed for fast reading it distills the larger essay into a compact institutional view of what already exists what is currently being stewarded where the main risks sit what a host institution would actually be taking on a pi administrator or collaborator should be able to see in a few minutes that sutton signwriting already has real systems real assets and real support needs snapshot summary sutton signwriting is not a greenfield initiative it already exists as a coordinated infrastructure across domains and public websites servers and platform systems software and code repositories datasets and corpus assets standards and encoding systems the central institutional task is not invention in plain terms it is stabilization documentation and institutionalization that means keeping existing systems running documenting them clearly and giving them stronger institutional support asset classes domains and public web presence current maintenance includes a portfolio of domains such as signwriting signpuddle signbank sutton signwriting valerie sutton related archival project and personal domains these domains support public access application entry points documentation and standards archives and historical continuity servers and platform systems the ecosystem depends on legacy systems still carrying historical continuity active production systems static and dynamic hosting ongoing redevelopment through platform v3 these systems support websites dictionaries downloads developer resources software and code the software environment includes javascript python and php libraries rendering and font systems clients viewers and components apis and platform code this is a mature technical base with open source and production facing value data and corpora key data assets include signpuddle dictionaries spml files millions of sign entries lexical and textual materials educational and religious literature collections this is a rare corpus of written signed language with research and preservation value standards and encoding core standards infrastructure includes iswa 2010 formal signwriting in ascii fsw signwriting in unicode swu associated fonts and rendering systems together these form a stable machine readable framework for signwriting stewardship chain current stewardship already follows a clear lineage valerie sutton originator and foundational system design steve slevinski technical steward encoding architect and maintainer next generation continuity path identified but still too informal this means continuity exists but the support model is not yet strong enough for the scale of the assets involved immediate risk areas the main risks are structural concentrated operational knowledge dependence on very few maintainers incomplete documentation limited redundancy across systems and responsibilities preservation risk for domains data and services these are typical signs of infrastructure that has grown faster than the support around it what a host institution would be doing a host institution would not be funding an idea from zero being asked to invent relevance a host institution would be stabilizing existing infrastructure improving documentation and continuity supporting preservation and modernization making grants research use and collaboration more credible institutional reading the clearest institutional conclusion is this sutton signwriting has already crossed the line from interesting project to real infrastructure the remaining question is whether that infrastructure will remain dependent on an informal maintenance model or receive the stronger academic and technical home its current value now warrants bottom line already in operation domains servers software datasets standards now needed stabilization documentation institutionalization

records.searchText: language without sound evidence notes evidence notes for language without sound series the science of sutton signwriting language without sound evidence notes the science of sutton signwriting language without sound evidence notes evidence notes language without sound evidence notes brain networks pathway expansion literacy and the case for signwriting infrastructure this evidence notes document identifies what is firmly established what follows from it where the structural gap lies for signed languages what signwriting already demonstrates and where the evidence remains promising but incomplete it also gathers the core references behind the argument in one place i what is strongly established language is not tied to sound strength strong the broad scientific claim underlying the project is well supported the relevant findings include sign languages are established natural languages signed languages recruit core language networks in the brain phonology is not limited to sound early access to natural language matters whether the language is signed or spoken reading and language processing can be studied in both modality general and modality specific ways the strongest formulation here is human language depends on accessible linguistic input not on one privileged sensory channel this does not mean that actual language use is unembodied or that modality is irrelevant it means that human language is not reducible to sound and that core linguistic organization can be reached through different embodied modalities language development and literacy development are not the same event strength strong the evidence strongly supports a developmental distinction between early language acquisition later literacy acquisition language must first reach the learner in an accessible form literacy then expands that language system by adding durable symbolic routes for reading and writing the strongest supported statement here is literacy builds on language it does not substitute for missing language access literacy is a learned symbolic expansion strength strong the literacy literature supports the following claims reading is learned rather than innate print reading recruits and reorganizes existing perception language systems braille demonstrates that literacy can be built through touch durable symbol systems support revision comparison reuse and accumulation in ways recordings alone do not this matters because the argument about signwriting is not only about representation it is about what becomes possible when a language acquires durable symbolic pathways ii what follows from that science if the previous claims are accepted several consequences follow pathway type matters more than modality prestige the scientifically useful distinction is not between spoken language as normal all other forms as deviations the better distinction is between pathway types language input pathways language output pathways literacy input pathways literacy output pathways this framework lets spoken signed and tactile cases be compared without presupposing that speech is the standard against which all else must be measured braille proves modality flexibility at the literacy level braille is especially important in this project because it demonstrates two things at once literacy does not require vision durable symbolic systems reorganize what the brain can do with language braille should still be described carefully it is typically a tactile writing system rather than a separate language but it remains one of the strongest demonstrations that literacy can be built through more than one sensory route writing changes use organization and scalability the literature on reading writing corpora annotation and computation all point in the same direction writable systems support repeated revision writable systems support comparison across time and speakers writable systems support search reuse and accumulation writable systems support structured data for science and technology this bridge from literacy to infrastructure matters because the same symbolic properties that support reading and writing also support corpora annotation search comparison and computation reading affordances research helps sharpen this claim the strongest additional point is not that one medium is always better than another it is that reading depends on a bundle of affordances such as navigation revisiting pacing annotation physical or spatial orientation within the text those affordances shape how readers compare passages build cognitive maps of a document and return to earlier material that matters here because the argument for writable sign is not only that a sign language can be represented symbolically it is that durable text changes the kinds of intellectual work that become routine so the careful formulation is literacy depends not only on symbol access but also on the affordances that support navigation annotation revisiting and structured reuse this strengthens the case for signwriting infrastructure without forcing a simplistic claim that paper is always superior to screens or that materiality alone explains reading outcomes iii where the structural gap lies signed languages are fully linguistic but often lack equally mature written pathways the clearest formulation is signed languages have often lacked widely adopted durable native facing written infrastructure comparable to the writing systems available for many spoken languages video is essential but it is not the same as writing strength conceptually strong video is indispensable for signed languages it preserves performance timing movement and expression extremely well but it remains time based and linear that makes it much weaker than writable text for tasks such as close editing repeated comparison searching structured annotation routine revision everyday literacy practice the clearest formulation is video preserves language well but it does not itself create a direct read write symbolic pathway comparable to literacy research transcription and everyday writing are not the same some sign language notation and transcription systems are excellent for linguistic description and researcher facing analysis that is a genuine scholarly strength but many such systems were not designed to function as ordinary reading and writing systems for routine native use the distinction is therefore transcription can support analysis writing systems can support literacy text production and structured reuse this distinction matters because the present argument concerns not only analysis but also durable reading and writing pathways iv what signwriting already demonstrates signwriting is a sustained object of research strength strong support includes dedicated research archive dedicated publications archive historical dissertation and thesis lists symposium archives spanning multiple years and countries international dissertation and thesis production across multiple languages and institutions core links signwriting research library signwriting research publications archive signwriting dissertations and theses archive signwriting symposium archive together these materials show durable scholarly presence across countries languages and institutional settings signwriting already supports writing system software and standards work strength strong support includes comparative work on sign language scripts unicode proposal and follow on technical work digital editor and tool development corpus construction sign language processing work machine translation using formal signwriting representations evaluation work designed for signwriting strings current standardization work through the ietf formal signwriting internet drafts this is one of the strongest parts of the case for signwriting as infrastructure rather than only pedagogy signwriting already supports a plausible infrastructure layer the best supported current claims are infrastructural rather than population scale educational signwriting already meets many of the criteria of infrastructure even without formal institutional support signwriting already provides or has already supported readable and writable sign text structured and searchable representations corpora and lexicons software tooling computational representation standards oriented technical work that is enough to justify describing it as infrastructure in practice not only as a notation proposal v what remains promising but still limited educational outcomes strength moderate to strong the literature supports feasibility actual use pedagogical relevance classroom experimentation small and medium scale educational benefit but the current record is still limited by small samples case based designs uneven outcome measures limited large controlled studies the disciplined formulation remains the literature supports feasibility use and scientific relevance more strongly than broad claims of large scale literacy superiority neuroscience of written signed language strength emerging the broader neuroscience base for signed language is strong the broader neuroscience base for reading and braille is strong the specific neuroscience of reading written sign systems is still developing this is an opportunity not a weakness in the overall framework it means the strongest current argument is signed language is fully language literacy is a real second developmental layer signwriting creates a plausible written pathway worth studying directly tactile integration touch belongs in the argument but with precision tactile signing is a language access pathway braille is typically a tactile writing system deafblind tactile language and literacy routes are real but more variable and less fully mapped than the strongest spoken signed and braille baselines the strongest scientifically supported statement is human language pathways can be reached through audition vision and touch more specific claims should be framed carefully it is well supported that blind braille readers recruit touch based and occipital reading networks it is well supported that deaf signers recruit core language networks through vision it is promising but less fully mapped how deafblind tactile language users recruit language networks across different tactile systems it is too strong to say that skilled reading simply eliminates phonological mediation the safer claim is that skilled reading can become less dependent on overt phonological mediation vi comparative cases and what they show the case structure is selective rather than exhaustive hearing child with spoken language and print literacy what it shows language develops first print adds durable literacy later evidence status strongly supported deaf child with early sign language what it shows language does not depend on sound full language can develop through vision evidence status strongly supported blind child with spoken language and braille literacy what it shows literacy does not depend on vision durable symbolic pathways can be tactile evidence status strongly supported deaf child with early sign language plus a written sign pathway what it shows the question is no longer only language access the question becomes whether signed language can gain its own durable reading and writing pathways evidence status conceptually strong large scale outcome evidence still limited language present in the environment but not fully accessible what it shows social availability is not the same as perceptual access later literacy cannot simply repair missing early language access evidence status strongly supported for deprivation risk supporting emergence cases homesign and nicaraguan sign language remain useful supporting comparisons they show resilience under constraint pressure toward language making the difference between individual communicative invention and shared community language growth they are best treated as supporting comparisons rather than central literacy cases vii what becomes possible once signed language is writable if signed language gains durable readable and writable pathways then the following become much more tractable cognition and neuroscience direct study of visual spatial reading comparison of visual tactile and spoken literacy pathways experimental work using controlled written stimuli literacy and education reading and writing instruction within signed language bilingual education questions durable teaching materials better comparisons between different literacy routes linguistics and language documentation textual comparison lexicon building annotation systems corpora and archival continuity ai and language technology machine readable sign representations datasets and evaluation methods translation and language modeling pipelines long term interoperability through standards the argument therefore does not stop at orthography it extends toward infrastructure viii working formulations the strongest framing lines remain the brain is waiting for accessible language not for one privileged sensory channel language comes first literacy expands it braille shows that literacy can become tactile video preserves language well but it does not itself create a direct read write symbolic pathway signwriting matters because it can add readable and writable pathways for signed language the literature supports feasibility use and scientific relevance more strongly than broad claims of large scale literacy superiority ix open research questions these questions are especially useful for future facing framing how does adding a readable and writable sign pathway change literacy development for signers what is the relationship between written sign representation and reading development how do tactile writing and tactile signing compare with visual sign reading at the neural and behavioral levels how can signwriting be used as an input output or intermediate representation in nlp systems what shared infrastructure would most effectively support corpora lexicons annotation and standards work how might written sign systems interact with modality specific and modality general language pathways in the brain what written supports are most effective for deafblind learners across tactile and mixed modality environments what kinds of datasets and evaluation metrics best capture visual spatial language structure in signwriting records.searchText: other sign language scripts and their conceptions comparison notes comparison notes for other sign language scripts and their conceptions series sutton signwriting infrastructure initiative other sign language scripts and their conceptions comparison notes sutton signwriting infrastructure initiative other sign language scripts and their conceptions comparison notes comparison notes other sign language scripts and their conceptions comparison notes other sign language scripts and their conceptions asl phabet hamnosys aslwrite and what they imply about written sign language these comparison notes record a compact comparison that can support institutional or collaborator conversations without turning the larger initiative into a script comparison project compact comparison asl phabet main conception linear alphabetic script institutional setting university connected educational setting support funding pattern school and academic collaboration vision establish an alphabetic principle for asl and support a bridge toward written english hamnosys main conception analytic notation institutional setting academic research environment support funding pattern scholarly and institutional support for transcription work vision provide a detailed cross linguistic notation for sign representation aslwrite main conception plane based script with early partial encoding experiments institutional setting outside formal academia support funding pattern community driven support vision sustain alternate spatial script design and handwriting culture signwriting main conception plane based writing plus technical infrastructure institutional setting internationally used but still under housed institutionally support funding pattern independent stewardship plus growing need for durable institutional backing vision function as real writing technical infrastructure and long term public continuity different communities different pressures one reason these systems should not be flattened into a simple list is that they emerged from different communities and different design pressures asl phabet is easiest to understand through educational and alphabetic framing hamnosys is easiest to understand through linguistic notation and representation aslwrite is easiest to understand through alternate script design and handwriting culture signwriting increasingly has to be understood through both script culture and infrastructure stewardship this is why the systems can coexist in the same conversation without all doing the same job why institutional setting matters institutional setting changes what a system can realistically become a system born in a school and university collaboration may stay closely tied to instructional goals a system born in academic notation research may remain strongest in transcription and analysis a system sustained by community adoption may prioritize everyday script culture and handwriting a system used publicly across countries software and standards eventually becomes an infrastructure and stewardship problem this is one reason signwriting cannot be reduced to a simple script comparison useful distinctions the following distinctions help keep the conversation disciplined script is not the same as notation plane based visible writing is not the same as linear encoded storage script code is not the same as encoding name educational use is not the same as shared public infrastructure handwriting tradition is not the same as software or standards stewardship word centered instructional use is not the same as a broad public writing ecosystem one additional distinction matters in discussions of signwriting formalized writing system is not the same as illustrative drawing these distinctions matter because many sign language discussions collapse them standards note on sgnw sgnw is the iso 15924 script code for signwriting in bcp 47 language tags a form such as ase sgnw identifies american sign language written in the signwriting script that is a script level statement it is not the same thing as naming the concrete encoding used to serialize the text for the standards stack in this project formal signwriting names the public computable model fsw and swu name the two canonical encodings of that model sgnw remains the script identifier used in language tags this is the clean way to say that signwriting has a standards position stronger than the other script conceptions named in this presentation without confusing script identity with encoding format how to speak about asl phabet the most useful tone is visible enough to acknowledge not central to the signwriting agenda one current answer to the question of how signed writing should be structured avoid letting it become the center of the infrastructure case how to speak about hamnosys the most useful tone is important notation name valuable for analytic representation different in purpose from an ordinary writable script this makes room for respect without confusion how to speak about aslwrite the most useful tone is another serious plane based script helpful for keeping handwriting and spatial writing alive as live design questions fair about the current technical limits partial coverage hand built strings and no mature editor or implementation path yet this allows a friendly and accurate acknowledgment handwriting note the handwriting question deserves to remain open and constructive signwriting already has handwriting traditions shorthand cursive block printing these traditions have never reached uniform mass adoption but they remain real and can grow further aslwrite is worth acknowledging partly because it reminds the field that handwriting culture is not a side issue bottom line this initiative does not need to deny the existence of other systems it only needs to keep its priorities clear acknowledge the landscape avoid reactive comparison as the central project build signwriting forward as durable writing and durable infrastructure that depends in part on speaking clearly about what signwriting already is a formalized writing system with defined encodings grammar search rendering and implementation pathways not merely a set of sign illustrations

documented signwriting ecosystem it is that south korea provides a current institutional proof point signwriting appears inside a public korean sign language dictionary associated with the national institute of korean language core factual basis the central public fact is the opening of the korean sign language nuri dictionary on february 6 2025 the project record and launch materials describe the dictionary as based on korean sign language corpus data publicly searchable inclusive of korean equivalent expressions and example information inclusive of sign form pictures inclusive of sign language characters shaped with deaf participation in the compilation process for the purposes of this brief the strongest use of those facts is south korea provides current public institutional evidence that signwriting can function inside dictionary infrastructure that formulation is strong enough without overstating the case why the korea case matters the korea case is strategically useful because it is recent public institutional lexicographic visible in interface form rather than only in private documentation this makes it different from a historical anecdote or a private software trial it also makes it different from the brazil case brazil provides stronger evidence of institutional density educational spread and research depth south korea provides stronger evidence of current official public dictionary presence what appears to be present in the dictionary workflow the public descriptions emphasize features that matter for infrastructure interpretation sign form search korean search sign language video menus and instructions sign language characters customizable screen display these features matter because they show that the work is not purely symbolic they point to prepared in signwriting through signpuddle online that point is especially important for infrastructure planning because it suggests that current korean institutional work is tied to existing signwriting tooling rather than to a separate isolated implementation what the korean launch reveals about infrastructure dependence the korea case is not only important because of what appeared in public it is also important because of what the launch reportedly required operationally project notes indicate that the korean team depended on signpuddle 2 on the legacy signwriting server to author signs for the dictionary under launch pressure large scale use appears to have exposed scaling limits in the older system especially around slower save behavior and overlapping save operations direct moving around those constraints that operational story matters because it shows that the korea case was not a symbolic endorsement resting on separate local tools it depended on live signwriting includes the signwriting in korea country page on signwriting org jongtae kim s 2024 signwriting symposium presentation jongtae kim s 2025 book on korean and sign language presented on the korea page with valerie sutton s commentary these materials strengthen the interpretation that korean signwriting work is not accidental or momentary they do not need to carry the whole argument the public composition in the signbox detailed location s37f to s386 more detailed location handling especially in sequence oriented contexts punctuation s387 to s38b sentence and passage structure those
